name: Al Grey
abstract: Al Grey (June 6, 1925 – March 24, 2000) was an American jazz trombonist who was a member of the Count Basie Orchestra. He was known for his plunger mute technique and wrote an instructional book in 1987 called Plunger Techniques.

interviews.title: Al Grey interviewed by Michael Woods, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Mitchell
about_quotes.evidence: we had a small group together... we had a sextet for a small time a couple of years... Al and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Mitchell interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, November 16, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Snooky Young and Gerald Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Henry Coker, Al Grey and Benny Powell were the trombone section.
about_quotes.doc_title: Snooky Young and Gerald Wilson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Bobby Hutcherson
about_quotes.evidence: I said, “I’m Bobby Hutcherson, and I’m going to be playing with Al Grey and Billy Mitchell here.” ... I’d like to present to you the Al Grey–Billy Mitchell Sextet with Al Grey, Bill Mitchell, Doug Watkins, and Bubba Hutchkins on vibes.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bobby Hutcherson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Butch Miles
about_quotes.evidence: Well I had an enormous amount of help. Because sitting immediately on my left was the fabulous Al Grey... Al Grey saved me... I don't think he ever got one wrong, either.
about_quotes.doc_title: Butch Miles interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 4, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Butch Miles
about_quotes.evidence: I’ll never forget it from the very first night I was on that band... with the Basie band... And Al Grey would just lean forward and call out the number of the tune.
about_quotes.doc_title: Butch Miles interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, October 9, 2020 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Jon Hendricks
about_quotes.evidence: I opened at the Blue Note with Clark Terry, Benny Golson, Al Grey, and Red Holloway to do that album that’s now out, called Boppin’ at the Blue Note.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon Hendricks — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
